---
title: "CascadeDecays and decayamplitude compatibility audit"
subtitle: "Kinematics, dynamics ownership, and amplitude-convention validation"
author: "CascadeDecays.jl compatibility study"
engine: julia
format:
  gfm: default
  pdf:
    documentclass: scrreprt
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    colorlinks: true
    geometry:
      - margin=24mm
execute:
  echo: false
  output: false
---

## Executive result

This audit compares `CascadeDecays.jl` at commit
`a372505560c4a8ebc52b5c44b0e15ac907e33476` with
[`KaiHabermann/decayamplitude` v0.1.9](https://github.com/KaiHabermann/decayamplitude/tree/39e0f1fb03778abb29ccab4bf09c497a2b1c34e3)
and its pinned kinematic dependency
[`decayangle` v1.1.2](https://github.com/KaiHabermann/decayangle/tree/a2920831474e06091dd9a6c21efac9af9461f643).

The central result is positive: for the default `decayamplitude` helicity convention,
the two frameworks use the same local helicity angles, standard Wigner
`D(phi, theta, 0)` convention, complex conjugation, Jacob--Wick particle-2
phase, LS-to-helicity transformation, and net `sqrt(2J+1)` factor per internal
line. A one-event computation-graph probe agrees at every checked layer to
better than `6e-16`.

The incompatibilities that matter for serialization are instead structural:

1. `decayangle` sorts children by default, while CascadeDecays treats written
   child order as physical.
2. `decayamplitude.DecayChain` owns an event and arbitrary Python lineshape
   closures; a CascadeDecays model is event-independent and receives a separate
   `KinematicPoint`.
3. `decayamplitude` has no inspectable lineshape or form-factor vocabulary.
   CascadeDecays separates a `propagator(s)` from a vertex form factor
   `ff(s0,s1,s2)`.
4. A `decayamplitude.Resonance` can sum several LS or helicity couplings at one
   vertex. A normal CascadeDecays vertex stores one recoupling term; importing a
   sum requires chain expansion or a new linear-combination vertex type.
5. Cross-topology Wigner alignment is automatic in `ChainCombiner` but opt-in,
   by external particle, in `KinematicTask`.

Consequently, serialization should target a versioned, framework-neutral
intermediate representation. It should not pickle Python callables, record
`Resonance.id`, or serialize Julia concrete type names as the physics model.

Two upstream API issues follow directly from the audit:

1. A propagator must be a first-class internal-line entity. It may own a table
   of decay channels, each with its own `l` and `s`, but its numerical contract
   is only `X(sigma | parameters)`. The active production or decay vertex must
   not inject LS/helicity labels or daughter masses into that evaluation.
2. A vertex form factor belongs to the vertex and is evaluated from the three
   masses of the lines it connects, `F(m0,m1,m2 | parameters)`, with the
   mass-versus-mass-squared convention and normalization declared explicitly.

The complete proposed issue texts are archived with this report in
[`decouple-propagators.md`](decayamplitude-issues/decouple-propagators.md) and
[`vertex-form-factors.md`](decayamplitude-issues/vertex-form-factors.md).

## Scope and method

The audit follows one event from four-vectors to a full helicity amplitude:

```text
RamboOnDiet four-vectors
  -> ordered topology and line invariants
  -> local helicity frames and (phi, cos(theta))
  -> LS coefficient x particle-2 phase
  -> conjugated Wigner D
  -> vertex factors
  -> internal-helicity sum
  -> propagators x sqrt(2J+1)
  -> external-helicity amplitude
```

The fixture is a massive three-body event generated with `RamboOnDiet`, seed
`0xDEC0DE`, parent mass `2.0`, and daughter masses `(0.4, 0.3, 0.2)`. The model
uses ordered topology `((1,2),3)`, external doubled spins `(0,1,1)`, root doubled
spin `2`, internal doubled spin `1`, root coupling `(2L,2S)=(0,2)`, isobar
coupling `(0,1)`, and unit lineshapes.

The checked inputs and outputs are in
[`event.toml`](fixtures/decayamplitude_compat/event.toml) and
[`reference.toml`](fixtures/decayamplitude_compat/reference.toml). The executable
probes are
[`verify_rambo_event.jl`](../scripts/decayamplitude_compat/verify_rambo_event.jl),
[`probe_cascade.jl`](../scripts/decayamplitude_compat/probe_cascade.jl) and
[`probe_decayamplitude.py`](../scripts/decayamplitude_compat/probe_decayamplitude.py).

From the repository root, the Julia side runs as

```bash
julia --project=docs scripts/decayamplitude_compat/verify_rambo_event.jl
julia --project=. scripts/decayamplitude_compat/probe_cascade.jl
```

The Python probe requires the pinned decayamplitude checkout or installation,
including `decayangle==1.1.2`, then runs as

```bash
python scripts/decayamplitude_compat/probe_decayamplitude.py
```

### Numerical closure

| Layer | CascadeDecays max error | decayamplitude max error |
|---|---:|---:|
| line masses and local angles | `0` | `5.38e-16` |
| representative graph terms | `0` | `3.33e-16` |
| all 12 external amplitudes | `0` | `2.24e-16` |
| spinful cross-topology alignment angles | `0` | `<2e-14` |
| all 12 aligned alternate-chain amplitudes | `0` | `<2e-15` |

For the component `(2h0,2h1,2h2,2h3)=(0,0,-1,1)`, the two internal-helicity
paths are:

| `2lambda_R` | root vertex | isobar vertex | product before spin normalization |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| `-1` | `-0.3992180457` | `-0.3779612865 + 0.3230365043i` | `0.1508889661 - 0.1289620019i` |
| `+1` | `-0.0853909753` | `-0.3822072381 - 0.3266654404i` | `0.0326370488 + 0.0278942806i` |

Multiplying their sum by `sqrt(2)` gives
`0.2595449794 - 0.1429313423i` in both frameworks.

## Compatibility matrix

| Concern | CascadeDecays | decayamplitude v0.1.9 | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| four-vector storage | `FourVector(px,py,pz; E)` | array `[..., (px,py,pz,E)]` | same |
| invariant stored/routed | squared mass `s` | daughter masses passed; parent `sqrt(s)` usually closure-captured | adapter needed |
| local angle | first ordered child | first ordered child | same after ordering |
| default local rotation | `conj(D(phi,theta,0))` | `conj(D(phi,theta,0))` | same |
| optional `minus_phi` | absent | `conj(D(phi,theta,-phi))` | unsupported variant |
| particle-2 helicity phase | exact `(-1)^(j2-lambda2)` | same expression | same |
| particle-2 frame transport | explicit particle-2 instruction | `R_y(-pi)` on daughter 2 | same numerically |
| LS-to-helicity coefficient | `sqrt((2L+1)/(2J+1))` and two CGs | same | same |
| internal spin normalization | explicit product `sqrt(2J_R+1)` | recursive factors with root cancellation | same net result |
| child ordering | written order is physical | sorted by default | different, high risk |
| event ownership | separate model and kinematic point | momenta stored in chain | different |
| lineshape interface | typed callable of parent `s` | arbitrary callable of `(2L,2S,params,...)` | different |
| form factor | separate vertex callable | normally folded into lineshape callback | different |
| root dynamics | no root propagator | root is a `Resonance` and its lineshape is evaluated | different |
| multiple LS terms per vertex | one ordinary recoupling term | coherent dictionary sum | different |
| topology alignment | requested external spins only | automatic for all final axes in combiner | activation differs |
| amplitude container | dense array, finals then root | dict keyed by final helicities; root is input | adapter needed |
| unpolarized intensity | sum over all axes, no mother average | same, no mother average | same |
| event batching | one `KinematicPoint` per event | JAX/NumPy arrays may carry batch axes | performance/API difference |
| identical-particle handling | explicit chains and shared coefficients | `Particle.type_id` influences sorting/topology generation | different |
| stable model identity | value-like topology/payloads | process-global creation-order resonance IDs | different |

## Part 1: kinematic variables

### Four-vectors, masses, and angles

Both frameworks use `(px,py,pz,E)` and metric `(+,-,-,-)`. On an event already
in the mother rest frame, CascadeDecays `CurrentFrame()` and decayangle's default
`helicity` convention produce identical angles. For the fixture:

| Vertex | `phi` | `cos(theta)` |
|---|---:|---:|
| root `((1,2),3)` | `-2.5149425199858495` | `-0.2091643181654772` |
| isobar `(1,2)` | `1.4144112448600463` | `0.011170731326575443` |

CascadeDecays stores one squared invariant per line. `decayamplitude` recomputes
masses from sums of final four-vectors, and its recently added callback path
passes daughter masses rather than squared masses. A neutral schema should use
`s` internally and state every callable's expected transformation explicitly.

### Child order is the largest kinematic hazard

CascadeDecays preserves bracket order. Thus `(1,2)` and `(2,1)` differ in local
angles, particle-2 phases, and frame paths.

`decayangle` defaults to `config.sorting="value"`: composite daughters precede
single particles and otherwise values are sorted. The constructed
`topology.tuple`, not the user's input tuple, is the physics topology. In
addition, [`ResonanceDict.stable_value`](https://github.com/KaiHabermann/decayamplitude/blob/39e0f1fb03778abb29ccab4bf09c497a2b1c34e3/src/decayamplitude/resonance.py#L301-L317)
sorts the flattened descendant set.

This does **not** make ordered amplitudes impossible in decayamplitude.
`decayangle` sorting can be disabled before topology construction (or replaced
by an identity ordering function). The resulting `Topology` retains ordered
`node.daughters`, and `DecayChainNode.amplitude` uses that order for angles,
helicities, the particle-2 phase, and recursion. Our numerical comparison uses
identical ordered topologies and confirms this path.

The remaining limitation is narrower: `ResonanceDict` uses an unordered
descendant set only to find a payload. A single dictionary therefore cannot
hold two different payload entries keyed by `(1,2)` and `(2,1)`. This matters
only when the payload itself is orientation-specific. It can be worked around
today by constructing per-chain resonance dictionaries, but a general adapter
should address payloads by a topology-local ordered vertex ID rather than by
`ResonanceDict.stable_value`.

For example, an adapter can preserve an input topology as follows:

```python
from decayangle.config import config as decayangle_config
from decayangle.decay_topology import Topology

decayangle_config.sorting = "off"  # set before constructing any Node/Topology
topology = Topology(0, decay_topology=((2, 1), 3))
assert topology.tuple == ((2, 1), 3)
```

An exporter must therefore:

1. serialize `Topology.tuple` after construction;
2. write ordered `children=[child1,child2]` explicitly at every vertex;
3. record the decayangle ordering policy for provenance; and
4. never recover child order from a sorted descendant-set key; and
5. use per-chain dictionaries or ordered vertex IDs for orientation-specific
   payloads.

### Initial frame and event lifetime

`decayamplitude.DecayChain` stores `momenta` and caches `helicity_angles`.
Lineshape factories in its examples also close over an event-dependent parent
mass array. Replacing or mutating the momenta after construction can therefore
leave cached or captured values inconsistent.

CascadeDecays keeps the model static. `DecayChainKinematics` or `KinematicPoint`
is constructed per event. `HelicityRootFrame()` handles general moving-parent
four-vectors, with a current-frame fallback when the mother is already at rest.
decayangle expects the start node to be at rest for its recursive boost path;
the caller normally invokes `topology.to_rest_frame(momenta)` first.

The serialized model should contain no event arrays. Event serialization, if
needed, belongs in a separate object with a declared frame.

### Cross-topology alignment

Both implementations calculate relative Wigner rotations by comparing the
helicity-frame paths of a final particle. `ChainCombiner` uses the first chain as
the reference and aligns later chains automatically. CascadeDecays requires
`KinematicTask(...; reference_topology=..., wigner_finals=(...))`; unrequested
axes remain unrotated.

The model schema must name a reference topology and list the external particle
IDs whose helicity axes are aligned. Importing a `ChainCombiner` should request
all spinful final particles in `wigner_finals`.

The fixture also checks an alternate `((1,3),2)` chain aligned to the
`((1,2),3)` reference on both spin-1/2 final particles. All 12 aligned complex
amplitudes agree within `2e-15`; the Euler-angle representatives agree up to
their expected floating-point and periodic ambiguities.

## Part 2: lineshapes and form factors

### The required entity boundary

The current `decayamplitude` API evaluates a callable called `lineshape` from
inside the vertex LS/helicity sum. That placement conflates three independently
serializable objects:

```math
\mathcal A_c =
\left[\prod_{R\in c} X_R(\sigma_R\mid p_R)\right]
\sum_{\{\lambda_R\}}
\left[\prod_{v\in c}
  H_v\,F_v(m_{v0},m_{v1},m_{v2}\mid p_v)\,D_v^*
\right].
```

The ownership and evaluation rules should be:

| Entity | Owns | Event-dependent numerical inputs | Must not know |
|---|---|---|---|
| propagator/internal line | analytic model, pole parameters, independently declared decay channels and their `l,s` | its own actual `sigma` | the active vertex term, helicities, connected event masses |
| decay channel | channel particles, nominal threshold masses, channel `l,s`, channel parameters | none unless the analytic model explicitly defines otherwise | which vertex term happened to request the propagator |
| vertex | ordered parent/child line IDs, helicity or LS couplings, form factor and its normalization | the connected `(m0,m1,m2)` and angles | an internal-line denominator |

Thus "the propagator knows its channels" does **not** mean
`propagator(l,s,d1_mass,d2_mass,...)`. It means that channel records are part of
the propagator's declarative configuration. Evaluation is still
`X(sigma | parameters)`, and the channel sum or running-width policy is internal
to that model. Conversely, `F(m0,m1,m2 | parameters)` is a vertex factor because
its breakup momentum is defined by exactly those three connected lines.

This separation also resolves the particle-order conclusion. Ordered child
identity is a property of the vertex. A nonsymmetric vertex form factor receives
`m1` and `m2` in that stored order. A propagator does not use vertex child order
at call time; any ordered or unordered channel-particle convention is declared
inside its own channel record. Therefore particle order can be made exact in
`decayamplitude`, but only after disabling automatic topology sorting and
representing these entities separately. The present arbitrary callback API does
not provide enough structure to repair or serialize the distinction reliably.

### There is no decayamplitude lineshape convention to deserialize

`decayamplitude.Resonance` accepts an arbitrary callable. For an LS model the
call is effectively

```text
lineshape(two_L, two_S, *parameters, d1_mass=?, d2_mass=?)
```

The `d1_mass` and `d2_mass` keywords are passed only when signature inspection
finds both names or `**kwargs`. The parent resonance mass is not a normal
argument. Examples capture it in a closure. This is executable Python state,
not a portable model description.

CascadeDecays separates two roles:

- an internal-line propagator receives the parent line's event-dependent `s`;
- a vertex form factor receives `(s0,s1,s2)` for its ordered parent and children.

For interchange, the schema should expose a named `mass_argument` convention.
The conceptual vertex interface above uses masses `(m0,m1,m2)`; the current
CascadeDecays callable receives squared masses `(s0,s1,s2)`. Conversion between
them is mechanical only when this convention is explicit.

Built-in `HadronicLineshapes.BreitWigner` stores pole parameters, nominal
daughter masses, ordinary orbital `L`, and radius. This distinction exposes an
important units trap: decayamplitude passes doubled `2L` to the callback, while
`HadronicLineshapes.BreitWigner.l` is ordinary integer `L`.

### Breit--Wigner sign and denominator

decayamplitude does not define a Breit--Wigner. Its examples use

```math
X_{example}(s) = \frac{1}{s-m_0^2+i\sqrt{s}\,\Gamma}.
```

CascadeDecays' standard dependency starts from

```math
X_{Cascade}(s) = \frac{1}{m_0^2-s-i m_0\Gamma(s)}.
```

With identical imaginary terms these differ by a global minus sign, absorbable
into one chain coefficient. The shipped forms are not otherwise identical:
`sqrt(s)` versus `m0`, and constant versus running width. At the fixture's
`s=1.9919876737`, `m0=1.5`, and `Gamma=0.2`, the decayamplitude example gives
`-1.76419-1.93009i`, while CascadeDecays' P-wave running-width object with
daughter masses `(0.4,0.3)` and radius `1.5` gives `1.84333+1.93558i`.

The recording test added with daughter-mass support even sets its local BW mass
to `d1_mass`. That is suitable for testing argument plumbing, but physically it
uses the first child's four-vector mass in the resonance denominator and must
not become a serialization recipe.

Every portable lineshape must declare its analytic `type`, sign convention,
parent invariant source, width formula, pole parameters, channel masses, and
analytic-continuation policy. Unknown callables must be rejected or exported as
explicitly nonportable extensions.

The root is another `Resonance` in decayamplitude, so its lineshape is evaluated
at the mother vertex. CascadeDecays has no propagator on the root line. A
constant root lineshape maps directly. A nonconstant factor can map to the root
vertex form factor when it is declarative and factorizes from the recoupling;
an LS-dependent or otherwise opaque production factor requires chain expansion
or a separate top-level dynamics field.

### Form-factor normalization

`HadronicLineshapes.BlattWeisskopf{L}(d)` returns the unnormalized threshold
factor `F_L(q)`. A CascadeDecays vertex multiplies exactly the callable value; it
does not divide by `F_L(q0)`. The built-in running width separately normalizes
its channel coupling at the pole, which introduces the appropriate
`F_L(q)^2/F_L(q0)^2` behavior inside the width.

At the fixture isobar mass, for `L=1`, `d=1.5`, and nominal daughter masses
`(0.4,0.3)`:

```text
F_1(q)       = 0.6758008537
F_1(q0)      = 0.7045380700
F_1(q)/F_1(q0) = 0.9592112655
```

Thus `F_L(q)` and `F_L(q)/F_L(q0)` are measurably different models. The neutral
schema needs a required normalization enum such as `raw`, `pole`, or
`explicit_reference`, plus the definition of `q0`. Because decayamplitude folds
this into arbitrary code, it cannot be inferred reliably.

### Event masses versus nominal channel masses

Both frameworks may compute kinematic vertex masses from the event. They should
not silently use reconstructed external four-vector masses as the nominal
threshold masses or in a pole-normalization denominator. The recommended
default is:

- parent `s`: event invariant of the addressed internal line;
- stable daughter masses in width and `q0`: serialized nominal particle masses;
- event daughter invariants: available only to explicitly dynamic vertex factors;
- any other choice: opt-in `mass_policy` recorded in the model.

decayamplitude applies `nan_to_num` to daughter masses before calling a
lineshape, silently replacing NaN and infinite values by zero. CascadeDecays
does not apply an equivalent model-level replacement; the selected lineshape
controls threshold and complex-continuation behavior. A serializer should store
the numerical-domain policy and adapters should reject invalid event masses by
default rather than silently changing them.

## Part 3: amplitude construction

### Shared conventions, proven numerically

Both default amplitudes use

```math
V^{\lambda_0\lambda_1\lambda_2} =
D^{J_0*}_{\lambda_0,\lambda_1-\lambda_2}(\phi,\theta,0)
(-1)^{J_2-\lambda_2}
H_{\lambda_1\lambda_2}.
```

Their Wigner implementation is the standard
`D=exp(-im phi) d(theta) exp(-im' psi)`, followed by complex conjugation in the
amplitude. decayamplitude's optional `minus_phi` mode instead uses
`D(phi,theta,-phi)` and must be marked as a separate convention.

The LS transformation is also identical:

```math
H_{\lambda_1\lambda_2}^{LS} =
\sqrt{\frac{2L+1}{2J+1}}
\langle J_1\lambda_1,J_2{-\lambda_2}|S,\lambda_1-\lambda_2\rangle
\langle L0,S(\lambda_1-\lambda_2)|J,\lambda_1-\lambda_2\rangle.
```

decayamplitude multiplies `sqrt(2J+1)` at every recursive node and divides the
root factor in `chain_function`; the net factors are exactly the internal-line
factors that CascadeDecays multiplies explicitly.

### Different computational organization

decayamplitude recursively generates internal helicities and returns a sum of
products. CascadeDecays builds dense vertex tensors, multiplies them into a
line-indexed tensor, and contracts internal axes. These are equivalent
evaluation strategies, not convention differences.

The model spaces are different:

- decayamplitude coherently sums an arbitrary dictionary of LS terms, each with
  its own complex coupling and LS-dependent lineshape, at one `Resonance`;
- its direct-helicity scheme stores arbitrary `(lambda1,lambda2)` couplings;
- a normal CascadeDecays `Vertex(RecouplingLS(...), ff)` stores one LS term;
- `CascadeDecay` then supplies one complex coefficient per complete chain.

Importing a multi-term decayamplitude vertex into current CascadeDecays can be
done by expanding the Cartesian product of vertex terms into separate chains.
The coefficient of each expanded chain is the product of the selected vertex
couplings. This is exact when every LS-dependent dynamic factor is represented
on the corresponding expanded vertex or line, but it may grow combinatorially.
A better long-term representation is a `LinearCombinationRecoupling` or an
explicit list of vertex terms in the neutral schema.

Exporting CascadeDecays chain coefficients into decayamplitude has a coupling
gauge freedom: a complete-chain coefficient can be placed at any one vertex.
The adapter should use a declared rule, for example `coefficient_anchor=root`,
and preserve a stable parameter ID so round trips do not move or duplicate fit
parameters.

### Model generation, parity, and identical particles

`MultiChain` forms the Cartesian product of candidate resonances at topology
nodes and filters combinations whose generated couplings are invalid.
`Resonance.generate_couplings` may materialize several parity-allowed LS states.
CascadeDecays normally receives explicit chains; `minimal_ls_decay_chain`
selects one allowed LS assignment. A portable file must store the selected LS
terms, not a request to "regenerate allowed terms", because generation order and
selection policy are framework behavior rather than physics data.

decayamplitude's `Particle.type_id` and `DecaySetup` use particle identity to
sort and reduce generated topologies. CascadeDecays examples represent
identical-particle permutations as explicit chains with tied coefficients.
Serialization should therefore store explicit ordered chains plus parameter
sharing. It may retain identical-particle groups as metadata, but should not ask
an importer to regenerate or deduplicate the topology list.

`Resonance.id` is allocated from process-global class state and changes with
construction and copying. Resonance names also participate in global parameter
sharing. Neither mechanism is a stable serialization key. Parameter identity,
display name, and sharing group must be separate explicit fields.

### Output layout and intensity

CascadeDecays returns a dense array whose axes are final particles in label
order followed by the root helicity. decayamplitude returns a dictionary keyed
by a tuple of sorted final-particle helicities, with root helicity passed
separately. Both unpolarized helpers sum absolute squares over initial and final
helicities and do not divide by `2J0+1`.

## Proposed neutral serialization contract

The portable object should be a declarative model, not an executable object
graph. A minimal versioned contract needs the following sections.

| Section | Required content |
|---|---|
| `schema` | name, semantic version, feature flags |
| `conventions` | metric, four-vector order, doubled-spin units, ordered-child rule, Wigner-D definition, conjugation, particle-2 phase, spin normalization, intensity averaging |
| `particles` | stable ID, name, `2J`, parity, nominal mass, final-state position or resonance identity |
| `topologies` | stable topology ID and ordered binary vertices; reference topology ID |
| `alignment` | external particle IDs to align and reference topology |
| `parameters` | stable parameter ID, value/default, real/complex, sharing group, optional bounds |
| `chains` | topology ID, complete-chain coefficient parameter, vertex and internal-line assignments |
| `vertices` | parent/child line IDs, coupling basis, explicit LS or helicity terms, form factor |
| `propagators` | analytic type, parent `s` source, pole parameters, channels, width formula, sign |
| `form_factors` | analytic type, ordinary `L`, radius units, momentum source, normalization and reference point |
| `extensions` | namespaced, explicitly nonportable framework-specific data |

A representative convention block is:

```json
{
  "angular_momentum_units": "twice_integer",
  "children": "ordered",
  "metric": "+---",
  "four_vector_order": ["px", "py", "pz", "E"],
  "helicity_frame": "jacob_wick_particle_2",
  "wigner_D": "exp(-i*m*phi)*d(theta)*exp(-i*mp*psi)",
  "local_euler": ["phi", "theta", 0],
  "amplitude_uses_conjugate_D": true,
  "particle_2_phase": "(-1)^(j2-lambda2)",
  "internal_spin_normalization": "product_sqrt_2J_plus_1",
  "initial_spin_average": false
}
```

### Import and export rules

For decayamplitude to neutral form:

1. materialize the post-sorting `Topology.tuple`;
2. replace process-global resonance IDs with semantic IDs;
3. separate event arrays from the model;
4. materialize every generated LS/helicity term and parameter reference;
5. recognize only registered lineshape/form-factor factories;
6. reject opaque closures unless a namespaced extension is explicitly allowed;
7. record `helicity` versus `minus_phi` and the reference topology.

For neutral form to CascadeDecays:

1. validate the convention block before construction;
2. preserve child order exactly;
3. map doubled LS `2L` to ordinary `L` only in dynamics objects;
4. construct event-independent propagators from parent `s`;
5. expand multi-term vertices or construct a linear-combination recoupling;
6. request Wigner alignment for every declared spinful external particle;
7. permute external axes to finals-then-root.

For neutral form to decayamplitude:

1. set `decayangle_config.sorting="off"` before constructing any topology, then
   verify that every constructed `Topology.tuple` equals the serialized ordered
   tree;
2. construct registered, named dynamics factories rather than closures;
3. place complete-chain coefficients according to the declared anchor rule;
4. convert parent `s` to `sqrt(s)` only at a callback boundary that requires it;
5. expose nominal daughter masses separately from event daughter invariants;
6. construct per-chain resonance dictionaries when two chains require different
   payloads for opposite orderings of the same descendant set.

## Validation ladder for adapters

Every supported model should pass these tests in order:

1. topology and ordered-child identity;
2. all line `s` values on one RamboOnDiet event;
3. every local `(phi,cos(theta))`;
4. each Wigner matrix element on used helicities;
5. raw LS/helicity coefficient before particle-2 phase;
6. coefficient after particle-2 phase and form factor;
7. each vertex factor;
8. every internal-helicity path before summation;
9. propagator and spin-normalization products;
10. all external-helicity amplitudes;
11. aligned amplitudes across at least two topologies;
12. a small event sample comparing complex amplitudes up to only explicitly
    declared global phase or normalization freedoms.

The checked fixture and probes implement levels 1--10 for one nontrivial chain.
They also implement level 11 with a second topology and two spinful external
particles. The next implementation milestone should add registered
Breit--Wigner and both raw and pole-normalized Blatt--Weisskopf cases.

## Prioritized conclusions

1. Do not change CascadeDecays' default `D`, particle-2 phase, LS formula, or
   `sqrt(2J+1)` normalization to match decayamplitude; they already match.
2. Make ordered child identity and post-sorting topology export the first adapter
   invariant.
3. Introduce a registered dynamics vocabulary before attempting general model
   serialization. Arbitrary callables cannot round-trip safely.
4. Separate nominal particle masses, event invariants, and pole-reference masses
   in the schema.
5. Make form-factor normalization mandatory, never inferred.
6. Add a linear-combination vertex representation or accept exact but potentially
   large chain expansion.
7. Use semantic parameter IDs and explicit sharing; never serialize
   `decayamplitude.Resonance.id`.
8. Treat `minus_phi`, nonconstant root lineshapes, and opaque callables as
   unsupported features until their mappings are specified and tested.
9. Refactor `decayamplitude` so propagators are internal-line entities evaluated
   only as `X(sigma | parameters)`, while their channel `l,s` metadata remains
   declarative and independent of vertex LS terms.
10. Make vertex form factors explicit objects of the connected
    `(m0,m1,m2)`, with ordered children and normalization recorded in the model.

This is a source and numerical audit of the pinned versions above. Arbitrary
user-supplied Python and Julia callables can implement additional conventions;
those cannot be classified without registering and inspecting their semantics.
